Name: JARS-Quant
LongName: Journal Article Reporting Standards for Quantitative Research in Psychology
Description: APA's JARS-Quant master standard for reporting quantitative empirical studies in psychology and related behavioral, social, and educational sciences. It provides a core table of reporting expectations that applies regardless of design, with additional design-specific modules used alongside it.
Aim: To improve the clarity, completeness, transparency, and reproducibility of quantitative research reports in psychology by specifying the core information that should be reported across article sections regardless of specific study design.
ManuscriptSubType: Behavioral or social science quantitative report

Scope.Coverage: Quantitative empirical studies in psychology and related behavioral, social, and educational sciences, especially manuscripts prepared in APA style that may also require design-specific companion tables.
Scope.Focus: Core reporting of title, abstract, introduction, method, results, and discussion elements including hypotheses, sampling, measurement, design classification, data diagnostics, statistical analysis, participant flow, interpretation, and generalizability.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a qualitative reporting guide and not a stand-alone replacement for the design-specific JARS modules, SEM/Bayesian add-ons, or meta-analysis standards. JARS-Quant serves as the universal quantitative base table to be combined with more specific modules where needed.

primary.description: Appelbaum M, Cooper H, Kline RB, Mayo-Wilson E, Nezu AM, Rao SM. Journal Article Reporting Standards for Quantitative Research in Psychology: The APA Publications and Communications Board Task Force Report. American Psychologist. 2018. PMID: 29345484.

items.item_name: Identify the problem, variables, and populations clearly
items.item_question: Does the title, abstract, and introduction clearly identify the main variables, theoretical issues, populations studied, and problem under investigation?
items.item_question_background: JARS-Quant begins by asking authors to orient readers to the substantive problem, the focal variables, and the population before readers encounter the design and results details.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract how the manuscript names the main variables, target population, study problem, and central hypotheses or objectives in the title, abstract, and introduction.

source_page_refs.quote: Identify main variables and theoretical issues under investigation ... Identify the populations studied.

items.item_question: Does the abstract report objectives, participant characteristics, study method, findings, and conclusions with enough detail to understand the study at a glance?
items.item_question_background: The abstract is expected to summarize not only the question and findings but also the study method, sample, outcomes, and key statistical reporting elements.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract whether the abstract includes the problem, main hypotheses, participant description, design, sample size, materials, outcomes, findings, and conclusions.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe the study method, including ... Research design ... Sample size ... Outcome measures ... Report findings, including effect sizes and confidence intervals or statistical significance levels.

items.item_name: Specify hypotheses and relation to design
items.item_question: Are specific hypotheses, aims, and objectives stated, including how they relate to the research design and whether they are primary, secondary, or exploratory?
items.item_question_background: The revised JARS-Quant emphasizes separating primary, secondary, and exploratory hypotheses to help readers assess evidential strength and multiplicity.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the stated hypotheses or aims and note whether the manuscript distinguishes primary, secondary, and exploratory analyses and links them to the design.

source_page_refs.quote: State specific hypotheses, aims, and objectives ... Primary and secondary hypotheses; other planned analyses ... State how hypotheses and research design relate to one another.

items.item_name: Describe sample, measurement, and design fully
items.item_question: Does the method section report inclusion and exclusion criteria, participant characteristics, sampling procedures, sample size justification, measures, masking, psychometrics, and the applicable study design classification?
items.item_question_background: The core JARS-Quant method expectations are designed to let readers evaluate who was studied, how they were selected, what was measured, and which design standard should also apply.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract inclusion/exclusion criteria, demographics, sampling methods, power or precision rationale, measures and covariates, quality of measurement, masking, psychometrics, and the named design category.

source_page_refs.quote: State whether conditions were manipulated or naturally observed. Report the type of design as per the JARS-Quant tables.

items.item_name: Report data diagnostics and analytic strategy
items.item_question: Does the manuscript explain planned data diagnostics, missing-data handling, transformations, outlier processing, and the analytic strategy for primary, secondary, and exploratory hypotheses?
items.item_question_background: A major revision in JARS-Quant was the explicit inclusion of data diagnostics and analytic strategy before results are interpreted.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract planned exclusions, missing-data methods, outlier handling, distribution checks, transformations, and the inferential strategy for primary, secondary, and exploratory analyses.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe planned data diagnostics ... Describe the analytic strategy for inferential statistics and protection against experiment-wise error for primary hypotheses, secondary hypotheses, exploratory hypotheses.
source_page_refs.locator: Table 1 data diagnostics and analytic strategy rows

items.item_name: Report participant flow and statistical findings transparently
items.item_question: Does the results section report participant flow, recruitment timing, missing data, inferential statistics, effect sizes, confidence intervals, software, and any analytic problems or anomalies?
items.item_question_background: Transparent reporting of participant flow and the minimally sufficient statistical details is central to evaluating quantitative claims and reproducibility.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract recruitment periods, participant flow, missingness details, inferential statistics, effect sizes, confidence intervals, software, and any convergence or diagnostic problems.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide information detailing the statistical and data-analytic methods employed ... Results of all inferential tests conducted ... Effect-size estimates and confidence intervals ... Identification of the statistical software used.
source_page_refs.locator: Table 1 statistics and data analysis row

items.item_question: Does the discussion address support or nonsupport for hypotheses, limitations and bias, similarity to prior work, generalizability, and implications for future research or policy?
items.item_question_background: JARS-Quant expects discussion sections to separate what the data support from what remains uncertain, while situating findings in prior work and broader applicability.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract statements about support for primary or secondary hypotheses, threats to validity, comparison with prior studies, external validity, and future implications.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide a statement of support or nonsupport for all hypotheses ... Discuss generalizability (external validity) ... Discuss implications for future research, program, or policy.

Aliases: Journal Article Reporting Standards for Quantitative Research in Psychology
EvidenceExamplesPositive: The manuscript cites JARS-Quant and separates primary, secondary, and exploratory hypotheses while reporting participant flow and data diagnostics.
An APA-style psychology article reports sample selection, psychometrics, masking, data diagnostics, and effect sizes in the core JARS-Quant structure.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A qualitative interview study that follows SRQR but has no quantitative analyses is not a JARS-Quant match.
A pure meta-analysis manuscript should map more directly to MARS than to the JARS-Quant base table.
Matching.Explain: Use JARS-Quant as the master psychology quantitative-reporting overlay when a manuscript presents original quantitative empirical data and follows APA-style reporting expectations.
